page.text: The marine environment means all living and non-living things that exist naturally in the Earth's seas, oceans, bays and estuaries. The marine environment has many <hyper id="fragile">fragile</hyper> <hyper id="ecosystems">ecosystems</hyper> such as coral reefs (the Great Barrier Reef), tropical mangrove forests (Florida Keys) and island <hyper id="habitats">habitats</hyper> (the Galapagos Archipelago). Many of these rare ecosystems are internationally protected. They are called "special areas" and they are listed in the <hyper id="imo">IMO's</hyper> <hyper id="marpol">MARPOL</hyper> convention. Different ecosystems, including <hyper id="vulnerable">vulnerable</hyper> marine habitats, are very important to our planet. Marine life is often at risk from human activities such as fishing and shipping, offshore operations and tourism. <hyper id="climate">Climate change</hyper> and <hyper id="greenhouse">greenhouse gases</hyper> also threaten the marine environment.

list:hypertext.item: Fragile: Easily damaged, spoilt or harmed (synonym: vulnerable)
Ecosystem: A natural unit consisting of all plants, animals and micro-organisms in that unit.
Habitat: An ecological or environmental area inhabited by a particular animal or plant species.
IMO: The International Maritime Organization or IMO is an agency of The United Nations dealing with maritime affairs at international level.
MARPOL: An IMO convention which aims at preventing the pollution of the sea by ships.
Vulnerable: Easily harmed or hurt (synonym: fragile)
Climate change: This term refers to major changes in the weather conditions on Earth, caused by natural events or human activities.
Greenhouse gases: These are gases such as carbon dioxide or methane which trap heat above the Earth causing the planet to warm up.
